title: CECS 460 — Hardware/Software Co-Design and Design Flow
learning_objectives: Explain how requirements and constraints drive hardware/software partitioning decisions
Apply the start-in-software heuristic and identify when a function should move to hardware or an accelerator
Describe the tape-out point of no return and why it makes hardware changes exponentially more expensive than firmware updates
Identify common co-design pitfalls (vague requirements, interface mismatches, version skew, over-optimistic accelerators)
Compare simple board-level modeling to industry co-simulation tools and articulate what risks each can catch
Evaluate firmware framework trade-offs between abstraction, automation, and low-level control

hardware.wiring: No external hardware required for this chapter.

steps.title: Chapter 11 — Introduction
steps.subtitle: Hardware, software, and the negotiation between them

content_blocks.text: If the rest of this course has been about what's inside a SoC, this chapter is about how you actually design one—or, more realistically, how you design a product on top of an existing SoC like the ESP32. Every serious embedded project is a negotiation between hardware (logic, accelerators, peripherals, power domains) and software (CPUs, RTOS tasks, firmware frameworks).

You've already made hardware/software co-design decisions in labs without calling them that: using the ESP32's AES engine instead of hand-coded AES in C; offloading sampling to timers, DMA, and I²S instead of busy-waiting in loops; letting the ULP co-processor wake the main cores only when needed. This chapter pulls those instincts into a structured flow.

content_blocks.heading: The co-design flow — five stages
rows: Define measurable targets before writing any code or RTL
Decide which functions run in software vs. hardware/accelerators
Estimate performance and energy; catch big mistakes cheaply
Write RTL and firmware; define hardware/software interfaces
Bring pieces together; debug mismatches; run CI on both sides

question.text: Which of the following best describes hardware/software co-design?
choices: Writing firmware that runs on a CPU without any custom hardware
Designing hardware blocks in isolation from the software that will use them
A structured process for deciding which functions run in hardware vs. software based on requirements, measurements, and constraints
Porting an existing C library to run on an FPGA
question.explanation: Co-design means hardware and software are designed together, not in isolation. The process starts with requirements, uses measurements to guide partitioning decisions, and keeps hardware/firmware teams aligned throughout.

steps.title: 11.1 — Requirements and Specification
steps.subtitle: Good co-design starts before anyone writes Verilog or C

content_blocks.text: Requirements drive everything. Consider a battery-powered ESP32 smart sensor node that measures temperature/humidity and reports data securely over Matter. Its requirements fall into three categories: functional (take a reading every 60 s, transmit over Wi-Fi/BLE/Thread, indicate status with one LED), non-functional (≥1 year battery life, BOM cost under target, <500 ms wake-to-send latency), and security (keys stored securely, no plain-text radio packets). From a co-design perspective these drive everything: energy and latency requirements push you toward hardware radio blocks, hardware crypto, and deep sleep; cost constraints push you toward reusing existing IP rather than inventing custom silicon.

content_blocks.heading: Concrete vs. vague requirements
rows: "End-to-end latency ≤ 200 ms from sensor to cloud"
"OTA firmware update completes within 90 s over Wi-Fi"
"AES-128 encryption on every transmitted packet, key rotated every 24 h"

content_blocks.text: A good specification is concrete (measurable targets with units and thresholds), stable enough to avoid constant churn, and shared — hardware, firmware, and test all work from the same document. Documenting requirements early reduces scope creep and gives you a checklist for verification later.

question.text: A product manager writes: "The device should have good battery life." Why is this requirement unacceptable for co-design?
choices: It does not specify the battery chemistry or cell count
It cannot be measured, tested, or used to make a hardware/software partitioning decision
Battery life is a manufacturing concern, not a design concern
It should be phrased as a non-functional requirement instead
question.explanation: "Good battery life" has no units, no threshold, and no test condition. Without a concrete target (e.g., "≥1 year at one reading/minute on a 1000 mAh Li-ion cell"), the team cannot determine whether a ULP wakeup circuit in hardware is necessary or whether firmware-only deep sleep is sufficient. The vague requirement makes partitioning decisions guesswork.

content_blocks.text: Start in software unless you have a strong reason not to. Software is easier to change and patch, easier to debug (printfs, breakpoints, logging), and reusable across products and silicon revisions. You only start thinking about hardware when measurement shows that a specific operation dominates CPU time or energy — for example, AES/SHA for secure networking, a heavy FIR filter in a DSP chain, or a math-heavy control or ML loop. The questions then become: Can the CPU handle it at a reasonable clock and power? Are there existing accelerators (AES engine, DSP, I²S+DMA, ULP core) available? Is the product high-volume enough to justify custom hardware?

content_blocks.heading: ESP32 smart sensor — partitioning example
rows: Wi-Fi/BLE/Thread implemented in dedicated radio blocks
Crypto engines reduce CPU time and energy per packet
ULP samples periodically while main CPU is off
Complex, frequently updated, benefits from OTA patches
Decides sleep schedule; policy changes without a respin
Simple I/O; easily changed to accommodate UX feedback

content_blocks.text: Over-optimistic accelerator trap: Moving a function to hardware does not automatically make it faster end-to-end. If the time to move data into the accelerator (buffer copies, interrupt setup, DMA descriptors) exceeds the compute savings, you gain nothing. Always measure the full path, not just the compute kernel.

question.text: A firmware profiler shows that software AES-128 consumes 280 µs per call. The device encrypts one packet every 500 ms. Should this function be moved to the ESP32 hardware AES engine?
choices: Yes — any function that takes more than 100 µs should always be in hardware
No — 280 µs is only 0.056% of a 500 ms interval, so CPU headroom is not a problem
Yes — hardware AES is always more energy-efficient regardless of call frequency
No — the ESP32 hardware AES engine only supports AES-256
question.explanation: 280 µs / 500,000 µs = 0.056% CPU utilization. The software path is not a bottleneck at this call rate. The start-in-software rule says you only move to hardware when a specific operation demonstrably dominates CPU time or energy — 0.056% does not qualify. If the packet rate increased to one every ~3–5 ms (approaching 5–10% CPU), offloading would become worth evaluating.

content_blocks.text: Tape-out is the moment a chip design is sent to a semiconductor foundry to generate photomasks and begin wafer fabrication. Any bug in the RTL is replicated across every die on every wafer. Fixing a hardware bug after tape-out requires a respin: new masks, new wafer runs, months of schedule delay, and millions of dollars at advanced nodes. This is why tape-out is called the point of no return. Software is fundamentally different: a firmware patch requires engineering effort but no new masks, and in many products it can be pushed over the air in days or weeks.

content_blocks.heading: Cost to fix a bug: hardware vs. firmware
rows: Push OTA update to all devices
Firmware avoids broken block; uses alternate path
New mask layers, limited RTL change

content_blocks.text: This asymmetry leads to a key guideline: functionality that is likely to change (complex protocols, UI behavior, business logic) belongs in software if performance allows. Functions that must be extremely fast, energy-efficient, or security-critical (crypto engines, safety shutdown logic, DRAM controllers) are good candidates for hardware — but only after very careful specification and verification.

question.text: After a chip tapes out, engineers discover that the hardware timer block has a bug causing a 0.08% timing error — well within the firmware's acceptable tolerance. A respin would cost $3M and delay launch by 8 months. What is the correct decision, and what principle does it illustrate?
choices: Always respin — hardware bugs must be fixed in hardware
Ship with a firmware workaround; the cost and schedule impact of a respin far outweigh a correctable 0.08% error, illustrating the tape-out point of no return
Cancel the product — hardware bugs at this stage are unrecoverable
Release a software patch post-launch and notify customers of the hardware defect
question.explanation: 0.08% timing error is negligible and firmware can absorb it. Spending $3M and 8 months on a respin for a tolerable error is economically irrational. This illustrates the tape-out asymmetry: firmware workarounds ship in weeks at near-zero cost, while hardware fixes are catastrophically expensive. The lesson for co-design: keep flexible/changeable logic in software, and only commit to hardware after careful verification.

steps.title: 11.2 — Common Co-Design Pitfalls
steps.subtitle: Traps that catch student projects and real SoC teams alike

content_blocks.text: Good co-design is as much about communication and process as it is about clever architectures. The same pitfalls appear in student projects and billion-dollar SoC programs.

content_blocks.heading: Common co-design pitfalls
rows: "We'll figure it out later" leads to conflicting assumptions and late surprises
Lock a concrete spec before any RTL or firmware is written
Different expectations for reset behavior, timing, or data formats at the HW/FW boundary
Write a shared register map / interface spec both teams sign off on
Fancy accelerator designed, but data-movement overhead kills the speedup
Benchmark the full path (DMA setup + compute + readback), not just the kernel
No status registers, counters, or trace; bugs become impossible to localize
Design observability in from day one — counters, interrupt status, loopback modes
HW and FW teams using different versions of a spec or register map
Single source-of-truth document in version control; generate headers from it

question.text: A hardware team adds a new AES accelerator. The firmware team writes a driver assuming the accelerator uses a 128-bit input buffer at offset 0x10, but the hardware team changed it to offset 0x14 in a last-minute RTL update. What pitfall does this represent, and what process would have prevented it?
choices: Over-optimistic accelerator trap — the accelerator should have been benchmarked first
Version skew / interface mismatch — a shared, version-controlled register map that both teams reference would have caught the divergence
Vague requirements — a more concrete performance spec would have locked the address
Missing debug hooks — a loopback mode would have revealed the wrong offset
question.explanation: This is a classic interface mismatch caused by version skew: the hardware changed without the firmware team knowing. The fix is a single source-of-truth register map (e.g., a YAML/JSON hardware description) committed to version control, from which both RTL parameters and firmware header files are automatically generated. Any change to the register map forces a deliberate update in both codebases.

steps.title: 11.3 — Modeling and Simulation
steps.subtitle: Sanity-check decisions before committing to RTL or complex firmware

content_blocks.text: Once you tentatively decide what's hardware and what's software, you want to catch big mistakes cheaply before committing. Industry teams use high-level modeling languages (SystemC, MATLAB/Simulink), transaction-level models (TLM), and co-simulation frameworks that run firmware on an instruction-set simulator connected to a hardware model. These are powerful but heavyweight. For this course, modeling can be much simpler: write pseudocode or a simple C/MATLAB prototype of the algorithm, estimate operations per sample/packet, run it on the ESP32, and measure timing and energy using timers and current probes. Even this simple setup can catch big architectural mistakes early.

content_blocks.heading: Course-level vs. industry-level modeling
rows: CPU bottlenecks, energy per operation, rough latency
Bus contention, cache effects, multi-block interactions
Architectural performance, bus bandwidth, block interactions
Detailed timing, power gating, silicon-specific behavior
Firmware-hardware protocol bugs, interrupt latency, DMA races
Slow — can't run full OS workloads; expensive licenses

content_blocks.text: Try ideas in a model or prototype first, then commit to expensive implementation steps. The underlying principle is the same whether you're writing a quick C benchmark on the ESP32 or setting up a full SystemC co-simulation: cheap experiments early save expensive respins late.

question.text: For a CECS 460 project you time a software SHA-256 hash at 320 µs per block on the ESP32. Packets arrive every 10 ms. Which conclusion is most justified by this measurement alone?
choices: Hardware SHA acceleration is mandatory — 320 µs is always too slow
320 µs / 10,000 µs = 3.2% CPU per packet — software is acceptable now, but moving to hardware SHA becomes worth evaluating if packet rate doubles or triples
The firmware is broken — SHA-256 should take under 10 µs on any modern MCU
SystemC co-simulation is needed before any partitioning decision can be made
question.explanation: 320 µs / 10,000 µs = 3.2% CPU utilization. That's not a bottleneck at the current rate, so the start-in-software rule says stay in software for now. But if the packet rate doubled to one every 5 ms, CPU load would hit ~6.4%, approaching the range where hardware SHA (typically 2–8 µs) starts making economic sense. Simple board measurement gives you exactly this kind of data-driven insight without any heavy tooling.

steps.title: 11.4 — Implementation and Integration
steps.subtitle: RTL, firmware, interfaces, and debug tools working together

content_blocks.text: After partitioning and modeling, hardware and firmware teams implement their respective pieces and bring them together. Hardware designers write RTL in Verilog/VHDL, synthesize to gate-level netlists, run timing/power/area analysis, and define the hardware/software interface: memory-mapped registers (control, status, configuration), interrupt behavior and handshakes, and reset behavior. Firmware developers write C/C++ (or Rust) on SoC-specific SDKs — for ESP32 that's ESP-IDF or Arduino-ESP32 with FreeRTOS — creating tasks for networking, application logic, sensing, UI, and power management.

content_blocks.heading: Hardware/software interface elements
rows: Addresses, bit fields, R/W/RO/WO semantics, reset values
Trigger conditions, priority, enable/mask bits, clear procedure
Buffer addresses, sizes, linked-list format, completion signals
Which registers reset to what value; soft vs. hard reset
C structs, inline accessors, HAL functions wrapping the registers

content_blocks.text: Continuous integration (CI) can build and test both RTL and firmware regularly, catching regressions when someone 'fixes' a block in a way that breaks assumptions elsewhere. Logic analyzers, JTAG, and on-chip trace are essential for debugging mismatches between intended register behavior and what the hardware actually does.

question.text: You are writing a driver for a custom DMA peripheral. The hardware spec says the 'transfer complete' interrupt is cleared by writing 1 to bit 3 of the STATUS register. Your driver reads the STATUS register, ORs in bit 3, and writes it back. Why is this approach risky?
choices: Writing to a status register is always forbidden in memory-mapped I/O
A read-modify-write on a status register can accidentally clear other pending interrupt bits that were set between the read and the write
The DMA controller does not support bit-field writes; the entire register must be written at once
The interrupt should be cleared in the ISR, not in the driver
question.explanation: This is the classic read-modify-write hazard on write-1-to-clear (W1C) status registers. If another interrupt flag is set between the READ and the WRITE, your OR'd write will also clear that flag before your ISR sees it, silently dropping an event. The correct pattern is to write the constant value with only bit 3 set (e.g., STATUS = (1 << 3)) — no read required. This is a real co-design pitfall: the hardware spec must clearly mark W1C bits, and firmware must follow the protocol exactly.

steps.title: 11.5 — Tools and Design Environments
steps.subtitle: Firmware frameworks, hardware tools, and the abstraction trade-off

content_blocks.text: Modern co-design leans heavily on tools. On the firmware side, high-level frameworks like PlatformIO and Zephyr provide board support packages (BSPs), build systems, dependency management, drivers, networking stacks, and RTOS integration — making it much faster to bring up working prototypes. On the hardware side, cloud-hosted and open-source tools support RTL editing, synthesis, simulation, and even place-and-route, enabling small teams or courses to explore realistic flows without huge license costs. These tools trade convenience for control: you get productivity and community support, but you may lose visibility into low-level details (exact memory layout, interrupt priorities, fine-tuned timing) and pay a bit in code size or latency.

content_blocks.heading: Firmware framework comparison
rows: Peripheral setup, common libraries, easy upload
Fine-grained interrupt config, register-level control, RTOS visibility
RTOS tasks, Wi-Fi/BLE stack, NVS, OTA, power management
Some register-level visibility; framework overhead in tight loops
Portability, community support, driver maintenance burden
Cross-platform BSPs, device tree, networking, power
ESP32-specific hardware features may be partially exposed

content_blocks.text: For CECS 460, frameworks are great for getting systems working quickly — but it is still useful to occasionally look under the hood so you understand what they are doing for you. Knowing when to drop to bare metal (e.g., when a framework's HAL overhead exceeds a tight timing budget) is a core co-design skill.

question.text: You need an ADC sampling routine that must complete in under 8 µs. Benchmarking shows the ESP-IDF ADC HAL call takes ~15 µs. What is the best approach, and what is one valid trade-off of that choice?
choices: Use Arduino analogRead() instead — it is faster than ESP-IDF
Write a bare-metal routine that directly programs the SAR ADC registers; trade-off: you lose the portability and future-proofing that ESP-IDF HAL updates provide
Switch to a faster MCU — the ESP32 is not capable of sub-8 µs ADC sampling
Use DMA to batch multiple samples; DMA has lower per-sample overhead than any software path
question.explanation: Arduino analogRead() wraps the same ESP-IDF calls and is likely slower, not faster. DMA batching improves throughput but does not reduce first-sample latency below 8 µs for a single conversion. The correct answer is to bypass the HAL and program the SAR_ADC registers directly — this eliminates framework overhead and can achieve sub-8 µs conversions. The trade-off: your driver is now tied to the ESP32 register map; it will not compile for other targets without a rewrite, and you must manually track any undocumented peripheral errata.

steps.title: Chapter 11 — Summary
steps.subtitle: Where architecture, engineering, and economics meet

content_blocks.text: Hardware/software co-design is not just a technical discipline — it is also an economic and organizational one. Getting it right is what turns a clever idea into a product that works in the field and that a company can afford to build.

content_blocks.heading: Chapter 11 key takeaways
rows: Concrete, measurable, shared — before any RTL or firmware
Start in software; measure; move hot spots to hardware only when data says to
Point of no return — firmware patches cost days, hardware respins cost months and millions
Version skew, interface mismatch, vague requirements, over-optimistic accelerators
Simple board measurements catch most big mistakes; industry tools catch subtler ones
Shared interface spec + CI + debug hooks (trace, status registers, loopback)
High abstraction = fast prototype; drop to bare metal only when HAL overhead is measurably too high

content_blocks.text: The patterns in Chapter 11 scale from a two-person ESP32 project to a 500-person SoC program. The tools get heavier and the stakes get higher, but the underlying questions are always the same: What must the system do? Where should each function live? What happens if I'm wrong — and how do I find out early?

question.text: Which sequence correctly orders the five stages of the hardware/software co-design flow covered in this chapter?
choices: Partitioning → Requirements → Modeling → Implementation → Integration
Requirements → Partitioning → Modeling → Implementation → Integration
Modeling → Requirements → Partitioning → Implementation → Integration
Implementation → Modeling → Partitioning → Requirements → Integration
question.explanation: Requirements come first — you cannot make sensible partitioning decisions without knowing what the system must do. Partitioning follows, then modeling validates those decisions cheaply. Implementation builds each piece, and integration brings everything together. Skipping or reordering steps (especially going straight to implementation before requirements are solid) is the root cause of most co-design failures.
